tweets.clean_text: Many different club goods can be created for different groups of people. If there is no value for members, such groups will cease to exist. This is why I love the cooperative model as a self-governing organization.
tweets.core_insight: Cooperatives survive only when they deliver real, member-driven value.

tweets.clean_text: Africans already collaborate, and they do so through cooperatives. We need to learn how to bring this model into the mainstream.
tweets.core_insight: Cooperatives are a native African collaboration system that needs formal recognition and scaling.

tweets.clean_text: The funny thing is that people already do this. Esusu and cooperatives are savings systems. This is why LAPO is strong at the bottom of the pyramid.
tweets.core_insight: Informal savings cooperatives underpin financial inclusion at the grassroots level.

tweets.clean_text: Once more people can pay for insurance either through cooperatives or through consumption-based models, healthcare delivery will become much easier.
tweets.core_insight: Cooperatives can be effective channels for healthcare and insurance financing.

tweets.clean_text: As I said earlier, insurance payments can happen through cooperatives, which many people already belong to, or through embedded consumption payments. These models have worked for life insurance and can work for health insurance as well.
tweets.core_insight: Existing cooperative structures can be leveraged for scalable insurance distribution.

tweets.clean_text: We often think of technology only as operational efficiency instead of an opportunity generator. A friend in Kenya runs a business serving transportation cooperatives (SACCOs), providing backend systems, IoT fleet management, and generating more income for the cooperatives.
tweets.core_insight: Tech can unlock new economic opportunities for cooperatives, not just efficiency gains.

tweets.clean_text: Very true. This is why we are starting a friends group for these kinds of investments. Cooperatives have historically thrived using this approach.
tweets.core_insight: Trust-based investment groups and cooperatives share the same structural advantages.

tweets.clean_text: In the past, many African founders did not realize that equity is real and valuable. They gave it away for cash, believing investors were offering value cheaply, when in fact it was the opposite. A few exits and secondary sales eventually made this clear.
tweets.core_insight: Equity literacy is critical; founders often undervalue ownership due to lack of exit experience.

tweets.clean_text: I once had regular chats with a founder who would donate to charity after each conversation and send me the receipt. It showed he valued my time and understood its cost. He had a kind soul. I hope you are doing well, Habeeb.
tweets.core_insight: Respect, reciprocity, and value exchange underpin healthy founder relationships.

tweets.clean_text: While driving this morning, I reflected on the many close calls with bankruptcy and poverty I have faced, and how unexpectedly something always appeared to turn things around. Most entrepreneurs and freelancers eventually become believers.
tweets.core_insight: Entrepreneurship often involves extreme uncertainty, resilience, and faith in unexpected outcomes.

tweets.clean_text: A trust layer connecting cooperatives and credit unions could allow value built in one system to be lent into the other. DeFi attempted this in crypto, but few have applied the technology to real African contexts. What we need is truly decentralized finance.
tweets.core_insight: African DeFi should be built on cooperative and credit union trust structures, not abstract crypto speculation.

tweets.clean_text: The money from that act paid our office rent and helped me recover after our company failed. It came from a good deed my father did twenty years earlier. This is why I help others when I can and once took many free meetings with founders before it began to be abused.
tweets.core_insight: Long-term trust and goodwill compound across generations and business cycles.

tweets.clean_text: He made less than ₦1 million profit on a ₦44 million deal. Although they promised more jobs, he lost interest. Instead, he leveraged the bank for long-term project support. They could not risk withholding payment because the systems were critical to their operations.
tweets.core_insight: Strategic leverage and system dependency can outweigh short-term deal profits.

tweets.clean_text: I was drawn to crypto because of community, and I lost money due to the greed of a centralized platform. Decentralization matters, which is why I still believe crypto was built for Africa. We just haven’t delivered the right use cases at scale yet.
tweets.core_insight: Decentralization aligns with African realities, but success depends on grounded, real-world use cases.

tweets.clean_text: Never envy others; comparison steals joy and blinds you to opportunities nearby. A friend exporting to Europe is funded by a cooperative of African migrants who chose collaboration over competition. Do more.
tweets.core_insight: Collaborative capital formation through cooperatives unlocks opportunities missed by individual competition.

tweets.clean_text: It is easy to see why many Nigerians struggle with comprehension: they often focus on who is speaking rather than what is being said, leading to insecurity-driven comparisons. I wrote the tweet below as a Nigerian citizen.
tweets.core_insight: Societal bias toward personalities over ideas undermines constructive discourse and policy understanding.

tweets.clean_text: Snakes are part of an ecosystem; they control rodents and do not attack people. When their food source is removed, they leave or die off. I noticed rubbish heaps suddenly appearing in Abuja due to poor waste management.
tweets.core_insight: Systems thinking matters: environmental and social problems are often caused by broken ecosystems, not isolated actors.

tweets.clean_text: African cooperatives need deep, patient research that is not driven by immediate profit. DFIs often impose foreign models that do not work locally. Many people underestimate how deeply embedded these organizations are.
tweets.core_insight: African cooperatives are complex institutions requiring long-term, context-aware research and capital.

tweets.clean_text: This was effectively point-of-sale financing, though not the type popular with VCs or widely studied by firms like McKinsey. African cooperatives can facilitate BNPL and manage credit risk better than formal retailers.
tweets.core_insight: Cooperatives outperform formal retail systems in embedded credit and risk management.

tweets.clean_text: Crypto represents humans redefining economics. Communities and cooperatives thrive in African countries where governance has failed because humans are inherently tribal. Crypto communities are economic tribes.
tweets.core_insight: Cooperatives and crypto communities succeed by aligning with human social and tribal behavior.

tweets.clean_text: Rotating savings models have saved many people from financial ruin. Cooperatives run these models frequently and support more small businesses than any other institution. The best African SME bank is the cooperative.
tweets.core_insight: Rotational savings and cooperatives are the backbone of African SME finance.

tweets.clean_text: The most valuable startup today was founded by ex-bankers who still work with banks. This is not foolishness but wisdom. Banking is more entrenched than people realize, and disruption narratives are naive.
tweets.core_insight: Enduring financial systems are transformed through collaboration, not antagonistic disruption.

tweets.clean_text: The more I observe, the more I realize that private equity investors are not leaving Nigeria. They invest decades ahead, aggregating capital from many small investors.
tweets.core_insight: Long-term capital in Africa is built through aggregation, patience, and institutional trust.

tweets.clean_text: Twenty years later, money invested in startups has outperformed property as the Naira and Rand were heavily devalued. These startups were US entities valued in dollars.
tweets.core_insight: Currency risk makes global, dollar-denominated investments attractive for African capital preservation.

tweets.clean_text: SocketWorks was the first venture-funded startup in sub-Saharan Africa, but there was no global tech media to tell the story. We relied on an IFC press release that did not travel far.
tweets.core_insight: African innovation has long existed but suffered from poor global storytelling and visibility.

tweets.clean_text: FDIC limits reflect lived experiences with failed banks. People are shaped by the financial trauma of their parents. After my FTX loss, I doubt my children will ever touch crypto.
tweets.core_insight: Financial behavior is shaped by generational memory and institutional failure.

tweets.clean_text: Looking back, crypto feels absurd without real use cases. Years of promises, scams, and speculation have produced little value. Africa needs useful crypto applications, not hype.
tweets.core_insight: Crypto in Africa must move from speculation to practical, value-creating use cases.

tweets.clean_text: Nobody truly thrives without the rule of law. People either take very high risks or only transact with low-risk individuals. Even cash withdrawals at POS are risky. When we ran a Super Agent network, I saw more chargeback fraud than at any other time in my life.
tweets.core_insight: Weak rule of law increases transaction risk and pushes markets toward informal trust-based systems.

tweets.clean_text: Nontraditional investors in African cooperatives will be the greatest force of change on the continent. Not governments, and not even VCs. Markets ultimately create and fund what works for themselves.
tweets.core_insight: Market-driven cooperative capital will shape Africa’s future more than state or VC-led models.

tweets.clean_text: Never be envious of others; comparison steals joy and blinds you to nearby opportunities. A friend exporting to Europe is funded by a cooperative of African migrants who chose collaboration over competition. Do more.
tweets.core_insight: Diaspora cooperatives unlock global trade opportunities through collective capital.

tweets.clean_text: This is about realities on the ground. You cannot have a healthy pre-seed ecosystem without a strong talent pipeline. People chase early signals of potential while much of the best talent has already left or is trying to leave.
tweets.core_insight: Startup ecosystems fail without deep talent pipelines; capital alone cannot fix this.

tweets.clean_text: I learned from him and Chife that ongoing services were more profitable than one-off systems supply to Nigerian institutions. I stopped chasing large contracts and focused on small, continuous support services that people genuinely needed.
tweets.core_insight: Recurring service models outperform large transactional deals in unstable institutional markets.

tweets.clean_text: African cooperatives have far simpler needs than DAOs built on Ponzinomics.
tweets.core_insight: Real-world cooperatives require practical tools, not speculative DAO structures.

tweets.clean_text: There is no such thing as cheap capital when you pay for it with equity. European DFIs are the only real source of cheap capital in Africa because it is effectively free. At this stage, Chinese operational knowledge is more valuable.
tweets.core_insight: Equity is expensive capital; concessional finance and operational expertise matter more in Africa.

tweets.clean_text: People are sharing videos of Africans asking for help from there.
tweets.core_insight: Migration and distress signals spread rapidly through social media, shaping perception and response.

tweets.clean_text: I agree. I would only pay for it monthly when I actually need it.
tweets.core_insight: Pay-as-you-use pricing aligns better with real African consumption behavior.

tweets.clean_text: A trust layer that bridges cooperatives and credit unions would allow value created in one system to be lent into the other. DeFi attempted this in crypto, but very few people have applied the technology to what actually works on the ground in Africa. What we need is truly decentralized finance.
tweets.core_insight: African DeFi should be built as a trust bridge between existing cooperative and credit union systems.

tweets.clean_text: Everything discussed can be done better by strong communities. Crypto culture caused some damage, but DeFi changed many fundamentals. Like AI, something significant will emerge that disrupts venture capital, driven by communities with their own capital and lived experience.
tweets.core_insight: Community-owned capital and experience will eventually outperform traditional VC-led models.

tweets.clean_text: The Ajo/Esusu model is interesting. Two ideas came to mind: what if contributions were held in FX or converted to a stablecoin to reduce devaluation risk? And what if nobody withdrew for 12 months, with each rotation invested into selected assets?
tweets.core_insight: Ajo can be modernized using FX, stablecoins, and asset-backed investment strategies.

tweets.clean_text: Gratitude reflects higher intelligence, while hubris signals ignorance. These values can be learned within the right institutions. I met a founder running an Ajo startup who shut it down due to fraud and greed.
tweets.core_insight: Ajo systems fail without strong institutional values and trust enforcement.

tweets.clean_text: In Togo, bank branches operate inside markets. There is a gap between formal banking and Ajo that something unique can bridge. A new retail financial arrangement is coming.
tweets.core_insight: Ajo sits between informal markets and formal banking, representing a major integration opportunity.

tweets.clean_text: I once said PiggyVest is the largest Ajo or Esusu platform in Africa without calling itself that. The difference is that nobody collects first; everyone is forced to save.
tweets.core_insight: Modern fintech products often replicate Ajo mechanics under different branding.

tweets.clean_text: My first paid Calendly session last year was with a founder who ran an Ajo platform in Nigeria before moving to Canada. He shut it down due to bad actors. That experience helped me understand why LAPO works.
tweets.core_insight: Institutional discipline is essential for Ajo systems to scale sustainably.

tweets.clean_text: Ajo is not for everyone. It is for serious people with stable income.
tweets.core_insight: Ajo is a savings discipline, not a universal credit product.

tweets.clean_text: What about those who collect last? The best Ajo models are savings-based, not opportunistic. If someone wants a loan, Ajo is not for them.
tweets.core_insight: Ajo is fundamentally a savings mechanism, not a lending system.

tweets.clean_text: Esusu is savings. Many small businesses rely on it, and cooperatives are formed to support it. Many people understood this long before fintech companies raised awareness.
tweets.core_insight: Esusu is a foundational savings system for African small businesses.

tweets.clean_text: Nigeria contributes to Global Fund, which should function like an Esusu payout, yet funds are mishandled. This highlights ongoing issues with donor agencies.
tweets.core_insight: Formal aid systems often fail to replicate the fairness of local Esusu models.

tweets.clean_text: I discussed this deeply with colleagues. If most aid money flows back to donors, what is the point? This is the core problem with DFIs and donor agencies.
tweets.core_insight: Top-down financial aid lacks the accountability found in cooperative savings systems.

tweets.clean_text: Whenever Silicon Valley creates a new financial product, I look for its African equivalent. AngelList rolling funds are essentially glorified Esusu run through cooperatives.
tweets.core_insight: Many Western financial innovations are repackaged versions of Esusu.

tweets.clean_text: Meet family members or join an Esusu.
tweets.core_insight: Family and Esusu remain primary safety nets for finance.

tweets.clean_text: Esusu is delayed gratification and discipline. Once you are creditworthy in the West, commerce works against that discipline.
tweets.core_insight: Esusu enforces savings discipline in ways formal credit systems do not.

tweets.clean_text: Esusu does not rely on perfect payment systems. Traders have always extended credit to each other.
tweets.core_insight: Trust, not infrastructure, underpins Esusu.

tweets.clean_text: Patreon is advanced Esusu taken to the extreme.
tweets.core_insight: Subscription and creator economies mirror Esusu principles.

tweets.clean_text: Esusu, family, and loan sharks are how small businesses are financed in Africa. Microfinance institutions have largely failed at this.
tweets.core_insight: Informal finance outperforms formal microfinance for small businesses.
